description: The Emergency Economic Stabilization Act of 2008 created the Troubled Asset Relief Program (TARP) to bail out the U.S. banking system following the collapse of Lehman Brothers. The initial $700 billion proposal faced intense public backlash, leading to a failed House vote in September 2008 before eventually passing in October 2008.

key_events.description: Lehman Brothers files for bankruptcy, triggering widespread panic.
U.S. House of Representatives rejects the initial TARP legislation.
President Bush signs the revised Emergency Economic Stabilization Act into law.
Treasury Department announces capital injections into major banks.

calibration_notes: Useful for measuring the disconnect between elite institutional necessity and intense grassroots populist opposition.
